Behaviour

When loading some packages in REPL, `REPLExt` throws an exception. 

### Expected behaviour

No exception thrown?

### Reproduce

To reproduce:
1. `julia-release --startup-file=no -t 1`
2. `using IJulia` (or other packages)

Packages that reproduce the bug:
- `IJulia`
- `Pluto`
- `CairoMakie`

Packages that don't reproduce the bug:
- `StaticArrays`
- `PackageCompiler`

### Version info:

```
Julia Version 1.11.1
Commit 8f5b7ca12ad (2024-10-16 10:53 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 16 × AMD Ryzen 7 4800H with Radeon Graphics
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-16.0.6 (ORCJIT, znver2)
Threads: 16 default, 0 interactive, 8 GC (on 16 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_NUM_THREADS = 16
  LD_LIBRARY_LOADPATH = /opt/wine-staging/lib64:/opt/wine-staging/lib:/opt/nvim-linux64/lib:/opt/containerd/lib:
  JULIA_CUDA_USE_BINARYBUILDER = true
  JULIA_DEPOT_PATH = /home/<my-user>/.cache/julia
```

### Extra information

- Loading the packages in non-interactive mode works just fine.
- In REPL, when the exception is thrown, load the package again to make it works (v1.11.1).

  %.inv38 = fcmp fast olt double %4, %"t::Tuple[7]_ptr.unbox"
  %5 = select fast i1 %.inv38, double %"t::Tuple[7]_ptr.unbox", double %4
  %"t::Tuple[8]_ptr.unbox" = load double, ptr %"t::Tuple[8]_ptr", align 8
  %.inv39 = fcmp fast olt double %5, %"t::Tuple[8]_ptr.unbox"
  %6 = select fast i1 %.inv39, double %"t::Tuple[8]_ptr.unbox", double %5
  ret double %6
}
```
This is already at the IR level, so I assume this is not just for a single processor type.

Using `max_fast` indeed leads to a slowdown for longer tuples:
```
julia> @b ntuple(Float64, 30) min_tuple(_)
4.076 ns

julia> @b ntuple(Float64, 30) max_tuple(_)
13.283 ns
```

following came up in a [Discourse thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fast-ways-to-check-if-an-element-is-in-a-vector-of-vector-of-elements/121598/18). Consider the code
```
has1(t) = mapfoldl(==(1), |, t; init = false)
f(v) = findall(has1, v)
g(v) = [i for i in eachindex(v) if has1(v[i])]

new_v(N) = [NTuple{N}(rand(1:100, N)) for _ in 1:1000]
```
The functions `f` and `g` do essentially the same thing. However, from some threshold on, `f` gets significantly slower:
```
julia> @b new_v(6) f(_)
1.337 μs (5 allocs: 624 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(6) g(_)
2.063 μs (4 allocs: 528 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(7) f(_)
13.274 μs (5 allocs: 656 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(7) g(_)
1.973 μs (5 allocs: 1.969 KiB)
```
This happens with Julia 1.11.1, 1.10.5 and master. Since the problem is present in Julia 1.10, I'm assuming that it's different from #55227. Maybe it's an LLVM issue that one cannot do anything about, but I want to mention it here anyway.

EDIT: If one defines `has1(t) = 1 in t`, then `f` is much slower than `g` for apparently all `N >= 2`, usually by a factor of around 6.

Packages that don't reproduce the bug:

- `PackageCompiler`

When loading some packages in REPL, `REPLExt` throws an exception. 

### Expected behaviour

No exception thrown?

### Reproduce

1. `julia-release --startup-file=no -t 1`
2. `using IJulia` (or other packages)

Packages that reproduce the bug:
- `IJulia`
- `Pluto`
- `CairoMakie`

Packages that don't reproduce the bug:
- `StaticArrays`
- `PackageCompiler`

code","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-24T02:13:58Z","updated_at":"2024-10-24T02:13:58Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The following came up in a [Discourse thread](https://discourse.julialang.org/t/fast-ways-to-check-if-an-element-is-in-a-vector-of-vector-of-elements/121598/18). Consider the code
```
has1(t) = mapfoldl(==(1), |, t; init = false)
f(v) = findall(has1, v)
g(v) = [i for i in eachindex(v) if has1(v[i])]

new_v(N) = [NTuple{N}(rand(1:100, N)) for _ in 1:1000]
```
The functions `f` and `g` do essentially the same thing. However, from some threshold on, `f` gets significantly slower:
```
julia> @b new_v(6) f(_)
1.337 μs (5 allocs: 624 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(6) g(_)
2.063 μs (4 allocs: 528 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(7) f(_)
13.274 μs (5 allocs: 656 bytes)

julia> @b new_v(7) g(_)
1.973 μs (5 allocs: 1.969 KiB)
```
This happens with Julia 1.11.1, 1.10.5 and master. Since the problem is present in Julia 1.10, I'm assuming that it's different from #55227. Maybe it's an LLVM issue that one cannot do anything about, but I want to mention it here anyway.
```
Julia Version 1.11.1
Commit 8f5b7ca12ad (2024-10-16 10:53 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 4 × Intel(R) Core(TM) i3-10110U CPU @ 2.10GHz

- `any(a)` is transformed to `any(identity, a)` instead of calling 2-argument `_any`, and analogously for `all`.  I think this has the following advantage: If somebody wants to implement `any` for a custom `AbstractArray` type, then it is now enough to define the 2-argument `any`. Currently also the 1-argument version has to be defined explicitly.

- The restriction that the predicate `f` for the 2-argument versions of `any` and `all` be of type `Function` has been removed. None of the other methods for the 2-argument versions of `any` and `all` impose this, and I see no reason to have it here.

- I've deleted the explicit definitions of the 2-argument versions of `_any` and `_all`. They are covered by the following line
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/39f2ad1e941fd092b906d60e8d23c004e8ee5b7e/base/reducedim.jl#L1007
in the `for` loop a few lines later. (I believe that it doesn't matter anyway. As far as I can tell, the 2-argument `_any` and `_all` were only used for the 1-argument `any` and `all` and nowhere {"action":"opened","number":55673,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55673","id":2049890451,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM56LtiT","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55673","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55673.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55673.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55673","number":55673,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"fast methods for `any` and `all` for `Bool` tuples","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The new methods allow for vectorization and are therefore much faster: With nightly, I get
```
julia> t = ntuple(Returns(true), 32); @b any($t)
2.440 ns

julia> t = ntuple(>(4), 32); @b any($t)
5.014 ns

julia> t = ntuple(Returns(false), 32); @b any($t)
22.961 ns
```
With this PR,
```
julia> t = ntuple(Returns(false), 32); @b any($t)
2.638 ns
```
As for other `Tuple` methods, this is only done up to length 32. Beyond that, the generic method is called. At present, the cut-off is 3 elements.

I find this quite useful. What you {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55690","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55690/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55690/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55690/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55690","id":2506447703,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6VZVtX","number":55690,"title":"tuples of symbols don't get vectorized","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-04T23:00:37Z","updated_at":"2024-09-04T23:00:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I have noticed that while tuples of bit integers (and `Char`) get vectorized, tuples with `Symbol` elements won't. See the example below. I'm not an LLVM expert, but I would think that vectorization should be possible in this case, too. Is it intended behavior that it's not done?
```
julia> f(t1, t2) = reduce(&, map(==, t1, t2));

julia> t = (1, 2, 3, 4); @code_llvm debuginfo=:none f(t, t)
; Function Signature: f(NTuple{4, Int64}, NTuple{4, Int64})
define i8 @julia_f_1742(ptr nocapture noundef nonnull readonly align 8 dereferenceable(32) %"t1::Tuple", ptr nocapture noundef nonnull readonly align 8 dereferenceable(32) %"t2::Tuple") #0 {
top:
  %0 = load <4 x i64>, ptr %"t1::Tuple", align 8
  %1 = load <4 x i64>, ptr %"t2::Tuple", align 8
  %2 = icmp ne <4 x i64> %0, %1
  %3 = bitcast <4 x i1> %2 to i4
  %4 = icmp eq i4 %3, 0
  %5 = zext i1 %4 to i8
  ret i8 %5
}

julia> t = (:a, :b, :c, :d); @code_llvm debuginfo=:none f(t, t)
; Function Signature: f(NTuple{4, Symbol}, NTuple{4, Symbol})
define i8 @julia_f_1748(ptr nocapture noundef nonnull readonly align 8 dereferenceable(32) %"t1::Tuple", ptr nocapture noundef nonnull readonly align 8 dereferenceable(32) %"t2::Tuple") #0 {
top:
  %"t1::Tuple[1]" = load atomic ptr, ptr %"t1::Tuple" unordered, align 8

  %1 = icmp eq ptr %"t1::Tuple[2]", %"t2::Tuple[2]"

  %3 = icmp eq ptr %"t1::Tuple[4]", %"t2::Tuple[4]"

  %5 = and i1 %4, %2

  %7 = zext i1 %6 to i8

PR makes three changes to the way `any` and `all` calls are transformed into 3-argument `_any` and `_all` calls for `AbstractArray`:

- `any(a)` is transformed to `any(identity, a)` instead of calling 2-argument `_any`, and analogously for `all`.  I think this has the following advantage: If somebody wants to implement `any` for a custom `AbstractArray` type, then it is now enough to define the 2-argument `any`. Currently also the 1-argument version has to be defined explicitly.

- The restriction that the predicate `f` for the 2-argument versions of `any` and `all` be of type `Function` has been removed. None of the other methods for the 2-argument versions of `any` and `all` impose this, and I see no reason to have it here.

- I've deleted the explicit definitions of the 2-argument versions of `_any` and `_all`. They are covered by the following line
https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/39f2ad1e941fd092b906d60e8d23c004e8ee5b7e/base/reducedim.jl#L1007
in the `for` loop a few lines later. (I believe that it doesn't matter anyway. As far as I can tell, the 2-argument `_any` and `_all` were only used for the 1-argument `any` and `all` and nowhere else.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55671/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":1,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55671/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2338314666","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55671#issuecomment-2338314666","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55671","id":2338314666,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6LX9mq","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-09T14:40:45Z","updated_at":"2024-09-09T14:40:45Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Do you want to move forward with this? new methods allow for vectorization and are therefore much faster: With nightly, I get
```
julia> t = ntuple(Returns(true), 32); @b any($t)
2.440 ns

julia> t = ntuple(>(4), 32); @b any($t)
5.014 ns

julia> t = ntuple(Returns(false), 32); @b any($t)
22.961 ns
```
With this PR,
```
julia> t = ntuple(Returns(false), 32); @b any($t)
2.638 ns
```
As for other `Tuple` methods, this is only done up to length 32. Beyond that, the generic method is called. At present, the cut-off is 3 elements.

I find this quite useful. omments":15,"created_at":"2014-12-30T07:55:03Z","updated_at":"2024-09-11T13:52:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"So this is an issue that I find after figuring out how the keyword arguments are currently implemented in Julia. It will probably not be an issue anymore if #2773 is implemented.

The document on methods says

> Methods are dispatched based only on positional arguments, with keyword arguments processed after the matching method is identified.

However, method dispatch actually behaves differently when doing a function call with or without keyword arguments. i.e.

``` julia
julia> function f(::Integer)
       2
       end
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> function f(::Number; kw...)
       1
       end
f (generic function with 2 methods)

julia> f(1)
2

julia> f(1; a = 2)
1
```

What happens here is that `f.env.kwsorter` only has one method defined and therefore when calling with keyword argument, `f(::Integer)` does not participate in method dispatch.

IMHO, there are several possible ways to fix it,
1. Fix the document to include this behavior. This should be the easiest fix but will probably make the whole keyword argument/optional argument/multiple dispatch more confusing especially for someone who does not know how it all works behind the scene. (It's already quite confusing/surprising that anonymous function does not support keyword argument for someone (like me) that expects python-like keyword argument implementation.)
2. Having an entry (that just throw an error) in `env.kwsorter` even for methods that does not take keyword arguments. This can also avoid the following confusing abuse of overriding method
   
   ``` julia
   julia> function f(::Number; kw...)
          1
          end
   f (generic function with 1 method)
   
   julia> function f(::Number)
          2
          end
   f (generic function with 1 method)
   
   julia> f(1)
   2
   
   julia> f(1; a = 2)
   1
   ```
   
   This is probably the easiest way to fix the code and is consistent with the best long term behavior.
3. Fix #2773 and let the method themselves handle keyword argument dispatch. Since #2773 is on `1.0` milestone, hopefully this will be eventually be implemented.

PR makes the following symbols from `Base.Broadcast` public:

- Symbols appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
AbstractArrayStyle
ArrayStyle
Broadcasted
DefaultArrayStyle
Style
combine_axes
combine_styles
flatten
instantiate
result_style
```

- Symbols appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
Unknown
```

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
materialize
```
I think it makes sense to make it public given that `Broadcasted` is also public.

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
DefaultMatrixStyle
DefaultVectorStyle
```
I think it makes sense to have them available as shortcuts. Maybe one should even mention them in the documentation instead of using `DefaultArrayStyle{1}`, for example.

EDIT: Making `DefaultVectorStyle` and `DefaultMatrixStyle` public leads to failing doctests because they now appear in the Julia output instead of `DefaultArrayStyle{1}` and `DefaultArrayStyle{2}`. However, they are never used in broadcast.jl. If one doesn't want to have them public, why are they defined at all? Let me know if you want to have them public. Then I adjust the documentation.

- The following functions have been left out although they have docstrings:
```
broadcast_preserving_zero_d
broadcast_shape
make_makeargs
newindex
_broadcast_getindex
```

- The following symbol (with docstring) is currently exported **without** appearing in the documentation:
```
BroadcastFunction
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2348869014","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54060#issuecomment-2348869014","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060","id":2348869014,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6MAOWW","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-13T12:42:19Z","updated_at":"2024-09-13T12:42:19Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Any news?

requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-09-07T18:16:17Z","updated_at":"2024-09-15T23:57:22Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/55711","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55711","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55711.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55711.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Keyword arguments are mentioned several times in the discussion of the recent `Fix{N}` PR #54653. However, support for them was dropped in the end. I don't know if that was to keep `Fix{N}` simple or for more fundamental reasons. I would like to propose `FixKw` as a way to do for keyword arguments what `Fix{N}` does for positional arguments. I think it should exist for the sake of completeness. It is also helpful because `map`, `reduce` and the like don't allow to pass keyword arguments to the function one uses.

I've chosen the name `FixKw` to resemble `Fix` although the "fixed" keyword arguments can still be overridden. I'm open to other names.

Please let me know what you think. If you like it, I can add tests and update `NEWS.md`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55711/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55711/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2351840861","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/55711#issuecomment-2351840861","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/55711","id":2351840861,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6MLj5d","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-15T23:57:21Z","updated_at":"2024-09-15T23:57:21Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Are you interested in moving forward with this?

It seems to me that this has made `append!` and in particular `preprend` slower:
```
julia> t = (1:2, 3:4, 5:6, 7:8);

julia> f1(v, t...) = foldl((v, w) -> append!(v, w), t; init = v);

julia> @b [1,2,3] append!(_, $t...) evals = 1
148.000 ns (6 allocs: 592 bytes)

julia> @b [1,2,3] f1(_, $t...) evals = 1
123.000 ns (2 allocs: 464 bytes)

julia> f2(v, t...) = foldr((w, v) -> prepend!(v, w), t; init = v);

julia> @b [1,2,3] prepend!(_, $t...) evals = 1
417.000 ns (12 allocs: 880 bytes)

julia> @b [1,2,3] f2(_, $t...) evals = 1
128.000 ns (2 allocs: 464 bytes)

```

working on my package SmallCollections.jl, I've noticed a significant slowdown in 1.11.0-rc1:
```
using SmallCollections, Chairmarks

sh = shuffles(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2)
# this is an iterator over all ordered partitions of 1:14
# into 7 2-element sets, plus a permutation sign

function f(sh)
    s = 0
    for (t, _) in sh
        # "t" is the ordered partition, "_" would be the permutation sign
        s += sum(length, t)   # the right-hand side is just 7*2 = 14
    end
    s
end
```
EDIT: `sh = shuffles(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2)` (6 arguments) is enough for a 5x slowdown.
```
julia> @b f($sh)
1.718 s (without a warmup)   # 1.10.4
9.277 s (without a warmup)   # 1.11.0-rc1
```
~~The functionality is not yet in the published version, nor in master.~~
You can use the latest published version (v0.3.0) of SmallCollections.jl.

   elements. `:compact` output should not contain line breaks.
   This flag may be ignored for 3-argument [`show(io, mime, x)`](@ref).
```
I guess that this is what `show` for arrays and dictionaries does at present (apart from passing `:compact` on to the elements). It would also keep readers from asking themselves what "compact output with line breaks" may look like.

As expressed in the referenced Discourse thread, I would personally find a compact (human-readable) 3-argument `show` version quite useful. For this reason I suggest to add
```
Nevertheless, users are encouraged to take the `:compact` flag into account
when writing 3-argument `show` methods for the `"text/plain"` MIME type.

```
using SmallCollections, Chairmarks

sh = shuffles(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2)
# this is an iterator over all ordered partitions of 1:14
# into 7 2-element sets, plus a permutation sign

function f(sh)
    s = 0
    for (t, _) in sh
        # "t" is the ordered partition, "_" would be the permutation sign
        s += sum(length, t)   # the right-hand side is just 7*2 = 14
    end
    s
end
```
```
julia> @b f($sh)
1.718 s (without a warmup)   # 1.10.4
9.277 s (without a warmup)   # 1.11.0-rc1
```
The functionality is not yet in the published version, nor in master. You need to check out the branch "slowdown".
```
pkg> add https://github.com/matthias314/SmallCollections.jl#slowdown
```

I should have pointed out that on x86-64 and i686 with [BMI2](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X86_Bit_manipulation_instruction_set#BMI2_(Bit_Manipulation_Instruction_Set_2)) the code uses the PDEP instruction (via `llvm.x86.bmi.pdep.64`). function g(sh)
    s = Ref(0)
    @inline foreach(sh) do (t, _)
        s[] += sum(length, t)
    end
    s[]
end
```

working on my package SmallCollections.jl, I've noticed a significant slowdown in 1.11.0-rc1:
```
using SmallCollections, Chairmarks

sh = shuffles(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2)
# this is an iterator over all ordered partitions of 1:14
# into 7 2-element sets, plus a permutation sign

function f(sh)
    s = 0
    for (t, _) in sh
        # "t" is the ordered partition, "_" would be the permutation sign
        s += sum(length, t)   # the right-hand side is just 7*2 = 14
    end
    s
end
```
```
julia> @b f($sh)
1.718 s (without a warmup)   # 1.10.4
9.277 s (without a warmup)   # 1.11.0-rc1
```
The functionality is not yet in the published version, nor in master. You need to check out the branch "slowdown".
```
pkg> add https://github.com/matthias314/SmallCollections.jl#slowdown
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783","id":2349819768,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6MD2d4","number":54783,"title":"bugs involving `IdentityUnitRange`","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-06-12T23:10:07Z","updated_at":"2024-06-12T23:10:07Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The code in range.jl was presumably written under the assumption that all ranges (or all arrays) are 1-based. With `IdentityUnitRange`, this is not true anymore, and this leads to problems. I have found the following issues: For
```
u = 2:4
v = Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:4)
w = 1:9
```
one gets:
```
julia> axes(u) == axes(v), u == v     # equal arrays must have equal axes
(false, true)

julia> w[v][2], w[v[2]]     # the documentation defines w[v][i] to be w[v[i]]
(3, 2)
```
This happens with Julia 1.10.4, 1.11.0-beta2 and master.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54783/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"opened","number":54824,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54824","id":1922850042,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5ynFz6","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","number":54824,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"allow non-identity ranges for `Slice`","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"My understanding is that `Base.Slice(r)` just indicates that the range `r` is a full axis of some array. This is used by `view` to speed up operations (linear indexing). Hence I would expect that `Slice` passes all relevant methods on to its argument range. However, for `axes` and `getindex` this is not the case. These two methods instead assume that the argument range is some identity mapping. Here is an example:
```
julia> r = 2:8
2:8

julia> s = Base.Slice(r)
Base.Slice(2:8)

julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 5)

julia> axes(r)
(Base.OneTo(7),)

julia> axes(s)
(Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:8),)
```
With this PR:
```
julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 6)

julia> axes(s)
(Base.OneTo(7),)

julia> axes(r), axes(s)
((Base.OneTo(7),), (Base.OneTo(7),))
```
I've come across this while working on my small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl). The starting point is the idea that one should get offset arrays easily via `view` once one has offset ranges. I'm trying to see how far this approach can be pushed. As far as I can tell, it actually works very well -- until one uses `Slice` to make operations faster. The branch [OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices) that uses `Slice` does not work with current Julia, but with this PR it does. I find it weird that `view` stops working with offset ranges if one combines them with `Slice`.

I don't know what the original motivation was for assuming identity ranges. Maybe I'm missing something. I've run the `subarray` tests with this PR and they worked fine.

For identity ranges this PR should not introduce any difference. Apart from passing `axes` and `getindex` on to the argument slice, I have removed the methods for `axes1` and `size` because the default method works. I've changed `reindex` to align with `getindex`. I guess that the separate `reindex` method for `Slice` could actually be dropped because in the method for `AbstractVector` the function `maybeview` boils down to `getindex` for There I mentioned that indices are not taken into account when comparing ranges, while for `AbstractArray` in general they do matter. (I'm not the first to notice this.) I stumbled upon this while working on the small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl), but I think this is relevant in general.

The purpose of the PR is to see if one can take indices into account when comparing ranges. I've run the `ranges` tests (with offset ranges added) and they worked fine.

If one wants to address this issue, then the first question would be where offset ranges would live in the type system. One approach would be to allow existing range types to have elements that are not 1-based. This is currently done with `Base.IdentityUnitRange`, which is a subtype of `AbstractUnitRange{Int}`. This is also done with this PR, and it is the approach currently taken by OffsetArrays.jl. A different approach would be to copy the whole type tree. For example, there could be `GeneralOrdinalRange` that contains `OrdinalRange` (1-based) and `OffsetOrdinalRange` (variable first index). This way all existing methods for 1-based ranges would continue to work.

As said above, this PR is only meant to see if including indices when comparing ranges does work, and maybe to get further feedback. In one commit I delete the method `==(r::AbstractRange, s::AbstractRange)` because I think it's redundant. It checks if the two ranges have equal elements, what is exactly what the general method for `AbstractArray` does (modulo checking that the axes are AbstractRange"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-06-12T23:10:07Z","updated_at":"2024-06-16T14:07:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The code in range.jl was presumably written under the assumption that all ranges (or all arrays) are 1-based. With `IdentityUnitRange`, this is not true anymore, and this leads to problems. I have found the following issues: For
```
u = 2:4
v = Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:4)
w = 1:9
```
one gets:
```
julia> axes(u) == axes(v), u == v     # equal arrays must have equal axes
(false, true)

julia> w[v][2], w[v[2]]     # the documentation defines w[v][i] to be w[v[i]]
(3, 2)
```
This happens with Julia 1.10.4, 1.11.0-beta2 and master.

EDIT: I've just noticed that while `IdentityUnitRange` has a docstring, it is not mentioned in the documentation. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824","id":2355779512,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM5ynFz6","number":54824,"title":"allow non-identity ranges for `Slice`","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-06-16T13:43:11Z","updated_at":"2024-06-16T15:12:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54824","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"My understanding is that `Base.Slice(r)` just indicates that the range `r` is a full axis of some array. This is used by `view` to speed up operations (linear indexing). Hence I would expect that `Slice` passes all relevant methods on to its argument range. However, for `axes` and `getindex` this is not the case. These two methods instead assume that the argument range is some identity mapping. Here is an example:
```
julia> r = 2:8
2:8

julia> s = Base.Slice(r)
Base.Slice(2:8)

julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 5)

julia> axes(r)
(Base.OneTo(7),)

julia> axes(s)
(Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:8),)
```
With this PR:
```
julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 6)

julia> axes(r), axes(s)
((Base.OneTo(7),), (Base.OneTo(7),))
```
I've come across this while working on my small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl). The starting point is the idea that one should get offset arrays easily via `view` once one has offset ranges. I'm trying to see how far this approach can be pushed. As far as I can tell, it actually works very well -- until one uses `Slice` to make operations faster. The branch [OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices) that uses `Slice` does not work with current Julia, but with this PR it does. I find it weird that `view` stops working with offset ranges if one combines them with `Slice`.

I don't know what the original motivation was for assuming identity ranges. Maybe I'm missing something. I've run the `subarray` tests with this PR and they worked fine.

For identity ranges this PR should not introduce any difference. Apart from passing `axes` and `getindex` on to the argument slice, I have removed the methods for `axes1` and `size` because the default method works. I've changed `reindex` to align with `getindex`. I guess that the separate `reindex` method for `Slice` could actually be dropped because in the method for `AbstractVector` the function `maybeview` boils down to `getindex` for `Slice`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2171737899","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824#issuecomment-2171737899","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","id":2171737899,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6Bchcr","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-16T15:12:56Z","updated_at":"2024-06-16T15:12:56Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"What would be wrong with extending `Slice` to other ranges? I find it very natural to use `view` with offset ranges to change axes. To give an example with the package OffsetRanges.jl mentioned above:
```
julia> using OffsetRanges

julia> a = [1 2 3; 4 5 6];

julia> r = OffsetUnitRange(0:1, 1:2)
0:1 => 1:2

julia> s = OffsetUnitRange(5:7, 1:3)
5:7 => 1:3

julia> b = view(a, r, s)
2×3 view(::Matrix{Int64}, 0:1 => 1:2, 5:7 => 1:3) with eltype Int64 with indices 0:1×5:7:
 1  2  3
 4  5  6

julia> b[0, 6]
2
```
To indicate that one covers axes completely, one would like to say
```
julia> b = view(a, Base.Slice(r), Base.Slice(s))
```
but this doesn't work at present.

understanding is that `Base.Slice(r)` just indicates that the range `r` is a full axis of some array. This is used by `view` to speed up operations (linear indexing). Hence I would expect that `Slice` passes all relevant methods on to its argument range. However, for `axes` and `getindex` this is not the case. These two methods instead assume that the argument range is some identity mapping. Here is an example:
```
julia> r = 2:8
2:8

julia> s = Base.Slice(r)
Base.Slice(2:8)

julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 5)

julia> axes(r)
(Base.OneTo(7),)

julia> axes(s)
(Base.IdentityUnitRange(2:8),)
```
With this PR:
```
julia> r[5], s[5]
(6, 6)

julia> axes(r), axes(s)
((Base.OneTo(7),), (Base.OneTo(7),))
```
I've come across this while working on my small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl). The starting point is the idea that one should get offset arrays easily via `view` once one has offset ranges. I'm trying to see how far this approach can be pushed. As far as I can tell, it actually works very well -- until one uses `Slice` to make operations faster. The branch [OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl#with-slices) that uses `Slice` does not work with current Julia, but with this PR it does. I find it weird that `view` stops working with offset ranges if one combines them with `Slice`.

I don't know what the original motivation was for assuming identity ranges. Maybe I'm missing something. I've run the `subarray` tests with this PR and they worked fine.

For identity ranges this PR should not introduce any difference. Apart from passing `axes` and `getindex` on to the argument slice, I have removed the methods for `axes1` and `size` because the default method works. I've changed `reindex` to align with `getindex`. I guess that the separate `reindex` method for `Slice` could actually be dropped because in the method for `AbstractVector` the function `maybeview` boils down to `getindex` for `Slice`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2172016141","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54824#issuecomment-2172016141","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54824","id":2172016141,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6BdlYN","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-17T01:41:10Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T01:41:10Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I cannot fix the failing tests in LinearAlgebra/adjtrans from within this PR. AbstractRange"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-06-16T14:04:28Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T11:53:31Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54825","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54825","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54825.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54825.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This PR is prompted by the issue #54783. There I mentioned that indices are not taken into account when comparing ranges, while for `AbstractArray` in general they do matter. (I'm not the first to notice this.) I stumbled upon this while working on the small package [OffsetRanges.jl](https://github.com/matthias314/OffsetRanges.jl), but I think this is relevant in general.

The purpose of the PR is to see if one can take indices into account when comparing ranges. I've run the `ranges` tests (with offset ranges added) and they worked fine.

If one wants to address this issue, then the first question would be where offset ranges would live in the type system. One approach would be to allow existing range types to have elements that are not 1-based. This is currently done with `Base.IdentityUnitRange`, which is a subtype of `AbstractUnitRange{Int}`. This is also done with this PR, and it is the approach currently taken by OffsetArrays.jl. A different approach would be to copy the whole type tree. For example, there could be `GeneralOrdinalRange` that contains `OrdinalRange` (1-based) and `OffsetOrdinalRange` (variable first index). This way all existing methods for 1-based ranges would continue to work.

As said above, this PR is only meant to see if including indices when comparing ranges does work, and maybe to get further feedback. In one commit I delete the method `==(r::AbstractRange, s::AbstractRange)` because I think it's redundant. It checks if the two ranges have equal elements, what is exactly what the general method for `AbstractArray` does (modulo checking that the axes are equal).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2173185728","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54825#issuecomment-2173185728","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825","id":2173185728,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6BiC7A","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-17T11:53:30Z","updated_at":"2024-06-17T11:53:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"So it seems that there are no fundamental problems with this PR. The only thing that "breaks" is that some tests compare ranges when they only want to compare their values. To make this easier, I have defined `values` for `Base.IdentityOffsetRange` such that it returns a 1-based range, like for all other ranges. (Maybe one should do this for a suitable abstract type and then define additional methods for ranges that are already 1-based. Then it would automatically work for `OffsetArrays.IdOffsetRange`, too.)

My main question now is: Do you want to proceed in this direction at all?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2173185728/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

One approach would be to allow existing range types to have elements that are not 1-based. This is currently done with `Base.IdentityUnitRange`, which is a subtype of `AbstractUnitRange{Int}`. This is also done with this PR, and it is the approach currently taken by OffsetArrays.jl. A different approach would be to copy the whole type tree. For example, there could be `GeneralOrdinalRange` that contains `OrdinalRange` (1-based) and `OffsetOrdinalRange` (variable first index). This way all existing methods for 1-based ranges would continue to work.

As said above, this PR is only meant to see if including indices when comparing ranges does work, and maybe to get further feedback. In one commit I delete the method `==(r::AbstractRange, s::AbstractRange)` because I think it's redundant. It checks if the two ranges have equal elements, what is exactly what the general method for `AbstractArray` does (modulo checking that the axes are equal).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2185007374","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54825#issuecomment-2185007374","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54825","id":2185007374,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6CPJEO","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-23T14:14:58Z","updated_at":"2024-06-23T14:14:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I did a force push, and this made the tests (in the checks) disappear. - Packages that don't have "PkgEval: ok" on JuliaHub, don't precompile or fail their own tests on master: Augmentor, AxisIndices, AxisKeys, HalfIntegerArrays, ImageBase, ImageFiltering, ImageTransformations, Images, Interfaces, Oceananigans, QuasiArrays, Raven, StaticArrayInterface, StaticNumbers, TensorCast

- Packages that **pass** their own test both with master and with this PR: ArrayInterface, BitBasis, BlockArrays, DiskArrayEngine, HePPCAT, InfiniteArrays, SparseArrays, Static

- Packages that pass their own test with master, but **fail** with this PR: OffsetArrays, PaddedViews.
  I think that for PaddedViews one only has to adjust two lines in the test script.

- Not yet evaluated: FFTViews, GLTF

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52669","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52669/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52669/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52669/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/52669","id":2060484328,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM560H7o","number":52669,"title":"strange results when searching docstrings by call signatures","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-12-30T00:59:01Z","updated_at":"2024-05-10T12:59:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Although it  appears not to be documented, one can search docstrings by call signatures. However, the results may be wrong if methods with type parameters are involved. Here is an example:
```
module M

export f

"doc for f"
function f end

"doc for f(x, y)"
f(x, y) = 1

"doc for f(x::T, y)"
f(x::T, y) where T = one(T)

end

using .M
```
Then
```
help?> f(1, 1)
  doc for f(x, y)
----------------------
  doc for f(x::T, y)
```
as expected (the docstrings for all matching signatures are displayed), but also
```
help?> f(1)
  doc for f(x::T, y)
```
which appears wrong.

A look at the entry for `f` in the `META` dictionary
```
julia> first(getfield(M, Docs.META))
Pair{Any, Any}(Main.M.f, Base.Docs.MultiDoc(Type[Union{}, Tuple{Any, Any}, Union{Tuple{T}, Tuple{T, Any}} where T], IdDict{Any, Any}(Union{Tuple{T}, Tuple{T, Any}} where T => Base.Docs.DocStr(svec("doc for f(x::T, y)"), doc for f(x::T, y)
, Dict{Symbol, Any}(:typesig => Union{Tuple{T}, Tuple{T, Any}} where T, :module => Main.M, :linenumber => 11, :binding => Main.M.f, :path => "REPL[1]")), Tuple{Any, Any} => Base.Docs.DocStr(svec("doc for f(x, y)"), doc for f(x, y)
, Dict{Symbol, Any}(:typesig => Tuple{Any, Any}, :module => Main.M, :linenumber => 8, :binding => Main.M.f, :path => "REPL[1]")), Union{} => Base.Docs.DocStr(svec("doc for f"), nothing, Dict{Symbol, Any}(:typesig => Union{}, :module => Main.M, :linenumber => 5, :binding => Main.M.f, :path => "REPL[1]")))))
```
shows that the call signatures `Union{}`, `Tuple{Any, Any}` and `Union{Tuple{T}, Tuple{T, Any}} where T` are registered. The third one probably causes the strange result above.

I have found the following possibly related docstring in the source code:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/2b2f5344138f2b50ee91132592733743f33c5358/base/docs/Docs.jl#L191-L209

As far as I can tell, the example is correct. However, in the light of my example above, the reason that `Tuple{T}` appears in `Union{Tuple{T, Any}, Tuple{T}} where T` seems to be that `T` is a type parameter and not that `f(x::T)` is a valid signature. I doubt that this was not intended.

is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. change"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-01T17:51:00Z","updated_at":"2024-05-12T22:59:59Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/54324","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This is an extension of #53824 (therefore also fixing #52669), where details on the issue can be found (and, related to this, a different area where the issue shows, namely when you try to include documentation for a particular method of a function, which is similarly impossible when type parameters are involved).
The fix proposed there had two drawbacks:
- with it, it is no longer possible to discriminate between two constructor functions that differ only in the type parameters. This is remedied with this PR by adding the parameters of the constructor as a unique type to the front of the method signature.
- with it, constructors must specify type parameters explicitly. The previous behavior is restored with this PR, which allows to have the type parameters unspecified.
  However, I completely agree with @matthias314's analysis that in this way, it is impossible to make the distinction between existing types and parametric types (which cannot be done well during macro deconstruction). This is also an issue in the code currently in Base; any type parameter will always be seen as parametric. For this reason, I'd rather see this whole behavior disappear; in the end, you cannot write Julia code like this. Removing the `if isempty` would prevent such a usage. A kind of middle ground would be to allow the current behavior, but only if the type is constrained (because this would not be possible for existing types).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54324/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54324/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2106401600","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54324#issuecomment-2106401600","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54324","id":2106401600,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM59jSNA","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-12T22:59:58Z","updated_at":"2024-05-12T22:59:58Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"[I'm moving the discussion out of the code review because it has a wider scope]

In my opinion, we should not allow the syntax `f{T}(x::T)` for docstrings if `f` is a function (as opposed to a constructor). For constructors, I also find it bad syntax because it cannot distinguish between actual types and type parameters. If `T` is a parameter, then one should write `f{T}(x::T) where T`. However, the currently allowed syntax might be so widespread in the existing documentation that it might be better not to change it. After all, replacing a type parameter by an actual type should not happen often. I still think that it would be helpful to run nanosoldier (or whatever tool is appropriate) over a large collection of packages to see how much would break with disallowing this syntax.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2106401600/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

My `startup.jl` contains the line `using InteractiveUtils`. Starting Julia 1.11.0-alpha2 with `--startup-file=no` works without problems. However, without that option I get
```
~$ /usr/local/julia-1.11.0-alpha2/bin/julia
┌ Info: Precompiling InteractiveUtils [b77e0a4c-d291-57a0-90e8-8db25a27a240] (cache misses: invalid header (10))
└ @ Base loading.jl:2845
┌ Warning: The call to compilecache failed to create a usable precompiled cache file for Markdown [d6f4376e-aef5-505a-96c1-9c027394607a]

be great if this could work:

```julia
julia> :(public foo)
ERROR: ParseError:
# Error @ REPL[13]:1:10
:(public foo)
#        └─┘ ── Expected `)`
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

Found because I was exploring `public` behaviors interactively and was trying to do something like the below — the equivalent using `export` works:

```julia
julia> @eval Base begin
       foo = 1
       public foo
ERROR: ParseError:
# Error @ REPL[16]:3:8
foo = 1
public foo
#      └─┘ ── Expected `end`
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51450/reactions","total_count":4,"+1":4,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51450/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2042724208","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51450#issuecomment-2042724208","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51450","id":2042724208,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM55wX9w","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-08T13:11:27Z","updated_at":"2024-04-08T13:11:27Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"What about adding this issue to the 1.11 milestone? - Symbols appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
AbstractArrayStyle
ArrayStyle
Broadcasted
DefaultArrayStyle
Style
combine_axes
combine_styles
flatten
instantiate
result_style
```

- Symbols appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
Unknown
```

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
materialize
```
I think it makes sense to make it public given that `Broadcasted` is also public.

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
DefaultMatrixStyle
DefaultVectorStyle
```
I think it makes sense to have them available as shortcuts. Maybe one should even mention them in the documentation instead of using `DefaultArrayStyle{1}`, for example.

- The following functions have been left out although they have docstrings:
```
broadcast_preserving_zero_d
broadcast_shape
make_makeargs
newindex
_broadcast_getindex
```

- The following symbol (with docstring) is currently exported **without** appearing in the documentation:
```
BroadcastFunction

PR makes the following symbols from `Base.Broadcast` public:

- Symbols appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
AbstractArrayStyle
ArrayStyle
Broadcasted
DefaultArrayStyle
Style
combine_axes
combine_styles
flatten
instantiate
result_style
```

- Symbols appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
Unknown
```

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, with docstring
```
materialize
```
I think it makes sense to make it public given that `Broadcasted` is also public.

- Symbols **not** appearing in the documentation, **without** docstring
```
DefaultMatrixStyle
DefaultVectorStyle
```
I think it makes sense to have them available as shortcuts. Maybe one should even mention them in the documentation instead of using `DefaultArrayStyle{1}`, for example.

EDIT: Making `DefaultVectorStyle` and `DefaultMatrixStyle` public leads to failing doctests because they now appear in the Julia output instead of `DefaultArrayStyle{1}` and `DefaultArrayStyle{2}`. However, they are never used in broadcast.jl. If one doesn't want to have them public, why are they defined at all? Let me know if you want to have them public. Then I adjust the documentation.

- The following functions have been left out although they have docstrings:
```
broadcast_preserving_zero_d
broadcast_shape
make_makeargs
newindex
_broadcast_getindex
```

- The following symbol (with docstring) is currently exported **without** appearing in the documentation:
```
BroadcastFunction
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2054155483","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54060#issuecomment-2054155483","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/54060","id":2054155483,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM56b-zb","user":{"login":"matthias314","id":56549971,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU2NTQ5OTcx","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/56549971?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314","html_url":"https://github.com/matthias314","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/matthias314/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-14T19:02:16Z","updated_at":"2024-04-14T19:02:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I've As a MWE, consider the following two functionally equivalent functions `f1` and `f2`:
```
function inc_index(v::NTuple{N,T}, i) where {N,T}
# increments the i-th coordinate of v by 1
    t = ntuple(Val(N)) do k
        ntuple(Val(N)) do j
            T(j == k)
        end
    end
    @inbounds v .+ t[i]
end

function f1(v, pp)
    for p in pp
        v = inc_index(v, isodd(p)+1)
    end
    v
end

function f2(v, pp)
    foldl(pp; init = v) do w, p
        inc_index(w, isodd(p)+1)
    end
end
```
On Julia 1.10.2 I get similar runtime for `f1` and `f2` with `Int16`, but 20x slowdown for `f1` and `Int32`.
```
julia> pp = collect(1:1000);

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
544.000 ns

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
485.576 ns

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
14.217 μs

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
490.224 ns
```
With Julia 1.11.0-beta1 I get:
```
julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
543.553 ns

julia> T = Int16; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
409.563 ns

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f1($v, $pp)
14.214 μs

julia> T = Int32; v = ntuple(Returns(T(0)), 8); @b f2($v, $pp)
14.074 μs
```

For Julia 1.10.2 and my Skylake machine I get:
`-O2` and `-O3`:
```
Int8: f1 = 537.593 ns, f2 = 340.635 ns
Int16: f1 = 561.471 ns, f2 = 481.950 ns
Int32: f1 = 14.242 μs, f2 = 488.390 ns
Int64: f1 = 4.231 μs, f2 = 7.137 μs
```
`-O1`:
```
Int8: f1 = 9.785 μs, f2 = 16.921 μs
Int16: f1 = 10.457 μs, f2 = 13.918 μs
Int32: f1 = 10.034 μs, f2 = 15.589 μs
Int64: f1 = 15.319 μs, f2 = 21.124 μs
```
Could it be that some `-O2` optimizations are missed or applied incorrectly?

Generally speaking, I would expect `f1` and `f2` to have identical benchmarks. This is the case for Apple Silicon.  However,  even among the "fast" timings above, there are significant differences. For example, both `f1` and `f2` are quite fast for `-O2` and `Int8`, yet `f1` is about 60% slower than `f2`.
